title: Need theories
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — Maslow, Herzberg, and McClelland need theories of work motivation.
learningObjectives: Outline Maslow's hierarchy of needs at work.
Analyse McClelland's need for achievement, affiliation, power.
Evaluate limitations of need theories in organisations.

simpleExplanation.title: What drives people at work
simpleExplanation.summary: Need theories argue workers are motivated when jobs satisfy fundamental needs — from pay and security to recognition and self-actualisation.

flashcards.back: Physiological, safety, belonging/love, esteem, self-actualisation — lower needs must be met before higher ones dominate.
Pay, conditions, supervision, company policy — prevent dissatisfaction but do not motivate.
Achievement, recognition, responsibility, advancement, the work itself — create satisfaction and drive.
nAch (achievement), nAff (affiliation), nPow (power) — people differ in which need dominates.
Sets moderate-risk goals, wants feedback on performance, prefers personal responsibility over luck.
Western individualist bias, hard to test Maslow's strict hierarchy, oversimplifies why people work.

sections.content: **Need theories** (4.1.1) opens Organisational Psychology. Compare **Maslow vs Herzberg** in evaluation — Herzberg splits factors into those that prevent dissatisfaction and those that create motivation.

sections.question: A call-centre raises basic pay and installs air conditioning but staff still report low motivation. Managers add a monthly 'top performer' award and let teams redesign their scripts. Using need theories, explain why the second package worked better.
sections.solution:
**Herzberg:** Pay and conditions are **hygiene factors** — they removed dissatisfaction (complaints about heat and unfair pay) but did not create lasting motivation. Awards and script autonomy are **motivators** (recognition, responsibility, meaningful work) that produce **job satisfaction**.

**Maslow:** The first package addressed **physiological/safety** needs only. Awards and autonomy target **esteem** and **self-actualisation** — but only after basics were met, consistent with the hierarchy.

**McClelland:** High **nAch** agents valued **personal achievement feedback**; team script design appealed to moderate **nAff**.

**Evaluation:** Need theories explain the pattern but **cannot predict individual differences** — some staff may still prioritise pay (hygiene) over recognition. **Cultural bias** in Maslow/Herzberg limits generalisation across collectivist workplaces.
